channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Stokes Chaos in Congress as He Huddles With the G.O.P.
item.description: Hours before visiting the Capitol, the president scrapped plans to sign a major housing bill, condemning “bad Republicans” for resisting his demands to ram through new voting restrictions.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Those attending the meeting at the Capitol on Wednesday said President Trump made a series of highly personal attacks against Republican senators who have bucked him.

item.title: Housing Package Passed by Congress Has Wide Appeal, but It’s No Quick Fix
item.description: The first housing package in decades will take time to put into effect, testing the patience of families rankled by high prices.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which cleared Congress on Tuesday, aims to increase new construction.

item.title: From ‘Terrible People’ to ‘Smart People’: The Trump-Led Right Rethinks Iran
item.description: The president has sought to recast the Iranian government as he pursues a peace deal. But there are signs that a softening on Iran in the Republican Party goes well beyond him.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran last month.

item.title: Iran’s Loyalists Promote a Wider Nationalism, Unveiled Women Included
item.description: Government supporters are showing off new ties with alleged former dissidents in a bid to show that they can withstand enemies at home as well as abroad.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran last month where some of the women were partially veiled and some dressed more conservatively.

item.title: Trump Asks Congress for $88 Billion, Mostly for War With Iran
item.description: The proposal was all but dead on arrival in the Senate, where it would need bipartisan support, and comes amid growing G.O.P. skepticism about the conflict.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has met privately with Republicans recently to make the case that the Pentagon urgently needed funding to buy additional weapons.

item.title: New Clues Point to Taylor Swift Wedding Event at M.S.G.
item.description: After weeks of speculation over Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s wedding plans, a permit has been filed for an event at Madison Square Garden over the July 4 weekend.
item.dc:creator: Emma G. Fitzsimmons, Maria Cramer and Dana Rubinstein
item.media:credit: Gregory Shamus/Getty Images
item.media:description: There has been intense speculation about the details of Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s wedding ever since they announced their engagement in August 2025.

item.title: Gay Marriage Is Dividing Republicans, Again
item.description: A look at the backlash to same-sex marriage — its strength, seriousness and the reasons behind it.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Zach Gibson/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court established same-sex marriage as a national right in 2015.

item.title: Support Builds on the Right for Prosecuting Women Who Get Abortions
item.description: Sentiment is shifting amid frustration in the antiabortion movement that more abortions are happening now than when Roe v. Wade fell.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Kitchener
item.media:credit: Michael Wyke/Houston Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Seth Gruber, an antiabortion activist who leads a group called “The White Rose Resistance,” has been circulating a petition among other activists to garner support for criminally charging women who get abortions.

item.title: As Blockbusters Loom, Monkey Business at the Supreme Court
item.description: An unlikely trio of justices issued a slashing critique of plea bargains that included several references to orangutans.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court still has yet to decide on several major cases, as its summer recess draws near.

item.title: After Bruising Primaries in New York, Democrats Look to November Elections
item.description: The party secured key victories in New York City, but battles across the state suggest a larger fight awaits in the fall.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes and Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cait Conley, right, will face Representative Mike Lawler in what may be the Democratic Party’s best chances of flipping a House seat.

item.title: NY Jewish Leaders Wrestle With Israel Critics’ Sweep in Primary Elections
item.description: Pro-Israel leaders in New York expressed alarm at the primary victories of three Democratic candidates backed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani. Progressive Jewish groups celebrated.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: What Alex Bores’s New York Defeat Reveals About the Fight Over A.I. Money in Politics
item.description: Alex Bores’s close loss in New York could pave the way for other Democrats to take political advantage of being attacked by the increasingly unpopular A.I. industry.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Laura Brett/Getty Images
item.media:description: Alex Bores with his wife, Darya Moldavskaya, on Tuesday night after he conceded his Democratic primary race for the House in New York City.

item.title: After New York’s Primary Elections, Democrats Face Five Big Questions
item.description: The anti-establishment left surged. How shaken are party leaders?
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York

item.title: Europe Heat Wave Latest News: After France’s Hottest Day Ever, U.K. Approaches June Record
item.description: More than a dozen countries have issued urgent heat warnings. France saw its highest average temperature ever on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar, Lynsey Chutel and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Why Is Europe the Fastest-Warming Continent
item.description: The burning of fossil fuels is raising temperatures worldwide, but local factors, on land and at sea, determine which regions warm most rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Toby Shepheard/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Morning commuters on the London Bridge on Wednesday, where temperatures reached 96 degrees Fahrenheit, the hottest recorded June day in the United Kingdom.

item.title: Visitors Look at the Reflecting Pool and Disagree on What They See
item.description: People have long gathered in solidarity by the Reflecting Pool, but amid the turmoil of President Trump’s attempted repairs there is little unity to be found.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The changing color of the water has been the subject of curiosity and controversy over the last two weeks.

item.title: Dianna Russini Was an N.F.L. ‘Insider.’ Was She Also Out of Bounds?
item.description: Ms. Russini’s closeness to a coach, Mike Vrabel of the New England Patriots, led to her breakup with The Athletic and its parent, The New York Times Company.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Rosman and Ken Belson

item.title: N.Y.P.D., F.B.I. Search Homes of Police Officials in Corruption Inquiries
item.description: The searches, by police internal affairs investigators and federal authorities, stemmed from earlier scrutiny of Jeffrey Maddrey, the former chief of the department, according to people with knowledge of the matter.
item.dc:creator: William K. Rashbaum, Maria Cramer, Chelsia Rose Marcius and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Heather Khalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Investigators searched the homes of current and former New York Police Department officials, including at this address on Manhattan’s Upper East Side.

item.title: Frank Carone, Longtime Eric Adams Associate, Is Arrested in Bribery Scheme
item.description: Brooklyn federal prosecutors accuse Mr. Carone and his brother of profiting from the city’s migrant crisis. He was the chief of staff to former Mayor Eric Adams.
item.dc:creator: William K. Rashbaum, Michael Rothfeld, Dana Rubinstein and Santul Nerkar
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Frank Carone, former chief of staff to Mayor Eric Adams of New York, was taken into custody along with three others on federal corruption charges. He is shown here outside Gracie Mansion after Mr. Adams was indicted in 2024.

item.title: France Identifies Its First Case of Ebola
item.description: A doctor who had traveled to the Democratic Republic of Congo was infected, the French health ministry said. The authorities said the risk to the wider population was low.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers last month in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of an Ebola outbreak that has killed more than 200 people.

item.title: The Ebola Outbreak’s Central Mystery: Where Did This Virus Come From?
item.description: Scientists believe that the Bundibugyo virus persists in an animal species, occasionally spilling over into humans. But they have yet to identify the species.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:credit: Michel Lunanga/Getty Images

item.title: The Wearable Data Your Doctor Actually Wants
item.description: Wearables can offer rough information about a person’s health, experts say, but only some metrics are medically useful.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times

item.title: A Billionaire’s Basquiat Collection Goes Up at Pérez Art Museum Miami
item.description: Ken Griffin, the hedge fund manager, looks for artworks with a “wow” factor, never mind the number of zeros on the price tag. Ten of his Basquiats are on loan to the Pérez Art Museum Miami.
item.dc:creator: Brett Sokol and Ysa Pérez
item.category: Perez, Jorge M, Art Museum of Miami-Dade County
item.media:description: Art handlers with Basquiat’s “Pez Dispenser,” from 1984, at PAMM. The show represents the collector Ken Griffin’s coming out as a major player on the Miami art scene.

item.title: Thomas L. Friedman on the Clash at the Core of the Iran Deal
item.description: How the world views of Jared Kushner and the founder of the Islamic Republic of Iran can help explain the issue at the heart of the negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Thomas L. Friedman and Daniel J. Wakin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; source photo by Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images

item.title: President Narcissus and the Fetid Reflecting Pool
item.description: Why the president’s latest renovation project is so irresistible and resonant.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Cottle
item.media:credit: Aaron Schwartz/Reuters

item.title: Shortage of Chemotherapy Drugs Brings Rationing Fears for Cancer Patients
item.description: Doctors are contending with low supplies and unfilled orders of generic chemotherapy infusions that are central to the treatment of a long list of cancers.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Emil Lippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chemotherapy drugs, carboplatin and cisplatin, in the pharmaceutical storage room at the Center for Cancer and Blood Disorders in Fort Worth. There are shortages of both drugs.
